skill.name: generating-rbs-inline
skill.description: Generates RBS-inline type annotations directly in Ruby source files using structured comments, with guidance for validation through rbs-inline, RBS, and Steep when available.
skill.summary: Add and validate RBS-inline annotations in Ruby source files.
skill.author: DmitryPogrebnoy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, script, and blocker patterns, but most reviewed hits are false positives from Markdown backticks, RBS syntax examples, and reference Zeitwerk source. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because its validation workflow instructs the agent to run local Ruby tooling and to edit Ruby source files.

medium_findings.title: Local Ruby Tool Execution During Validation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run rbs-inline, rbs validate, and steep check during validation. These are legitimate Ruby typing commands, but they execute local project tooling and dependencies, so users should review the project before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command instructions are explicit and semantically confirmed in the validation workflow. Risk is moderated because the commands are standard project-local Ruby typing tools, not hidden exfiltration or arbitrary shell construction.

low_findings.title: Reference Ruby Files Trigger Static Patterns
low_findings.description: Several flagged script and reconnaissance patterns occur in bundled Zeitwerk reference examples, including require wrapping, path expansion, glob expansion, and autoload management. These files are examples for annotation style, not install hooks or skill-executed scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is real Ruby, but it is located under reference examples and there is no evidence that the skill runs it. The behavior matches normal Zeitwerk loader internals rather than marketplace malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Syntax Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command and blocker hits are caused by Markdown inline backticks, fenced Ruby examples, and RBS syntax documentation. Reviewed examples describe annotations and generated type signatures rather than shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation and code fences, not executable skill code. The static pattern is explainable by backticks and Ruby examples in Markdown.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Sources
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to upstream documentation and example source references. No evidence found that the skill sends data to those URLs or performs network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plainly labeled as source references. I found no fetch logic, credential handling, or exfiltration flow tied to them.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project-Local External Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The validation process may invoke command-line Ruby tools inside the user project. This pattern is expected for a coding skill but should remain visible because project dependencies can affect command behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill directly names the commands and when to run them. The risk is contextual, not malicious, because the commands are scoped to type generation and validation.

content.value_statement: Ruby projects often lack inline type signatures, which makes maintenance and static checks harder. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to add RBS-inline comments and validate the generated RBS output.
actual_capabilities: Adds the rbs_inline magic comment to Ruby source files.
Annotates methods, attributes, constants, instance variables, classes, and modules with RBS-inline syntax.
Uses examples for Data.define and Struct.new annotation patterns.
Avoids regular .rbs files when the user wants inline annotations.
Guides validation with rbs-inline, rbs validate, and Steep when configured.
Reviews annotations to reduce untyped usage.
limitations: Requires readable Ruby source code with enough context for type inference.
May need user input for metaprogramming, dynamic values, or unclear return types.
Validation commands depend on local gems and project configuration.
Does not replace full application testing or human review of public APIs.

use_cases.title: Annotate a Ruby Library
use_cases.description: Add inline signatures to public classes and methods before publishing a gem.

use_cases.title: Prepare Code for Static Checking
use_cases.description: Generate RBS-inline comments and validate the generated signatures with existing tooling.

use_cases.title: Document Legacy Ruby Types
use_cases.description: Make method inputs, return values, attributes, and constants clearer in older Ruby code.

prompt_templates.title: Annotate One File
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single Ruby file
prompt_templates.prompt: Add RBS-inline annotations to this Ruby file. Preserve behavior, use # @rbs comments, and ask before using untyped.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Existing Annotations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these RBS-inline annotations, remove avoidable untyped types, and explain any type assumptions that remain uncertain.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Struct and Data Classes
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for Ruby value objects
prompt_templates.prompt: Annotate these Data.define and Struct.new classes with RBS-inline comments, including attributes, initializer behavior, and custom methods.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Typed Ruby Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Add RBS-inline annotations, generate RBS output, run the available validation commands, and fix annotation errors without changing runtime behavior.

output_examples.input: A Ruby class with readers, an initializer, and a greeting method.
output_examples.output: The file includes the RBS-inline enable comment, typed readers, typed initializer parameters, and a typed string return.

output_examples.input: A Struct-based value object with a custom method.
output_examples.output: The struct members have inline attribute types, and the custom method has parameter and return annotations.

output_examples.input: A Ruby file that already has partial RBS-inline comments.
output_examples.output: The annotations are normalized, unclear types are called out, and avoidable untyped entries are replaced.

best_practices: Review method bodies and tests before choosing parameter or return types.
Run validation commands only after checking the project dependencies and Gemfile.
Document uncertain type assumptions in the final response.
anti_patterns: Do not add regular .rbs signatures when the request is for RBS-inline comments.
Do not use untyped when local code context supports a more specific type.
Do not run project commands before reviewing what they will execute.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It generates RBS-inline comments inside Ruby source files, including method, attribute, constant, and variable annotations.

faq.question: Does it create separate RBS files?
faq.answer: No. The skill focuses on inline comments. Generated RBS files are only used for validation when the tool runs.

faq.question: Can it work with Steep?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide Steep validation when the project has the steep gem and a Steepfile.

faq.question: Will it run my Ruby application?
faq.answer: The skill tells the agent not to run application code. It may run typing and validation commands if appropriate.

faq.question: How does it handle unknown types?
faq.answer: It tries to infer specific types from code context and asks for more detail when the type is unclear.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
